title: Is There No Will? Is the Way Not Known?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. The Tamil section is a source-checked reading copy derived from the OCR and does not alter the canonical source file. Visual review restored the opening `உடன்பிறப்பே,`, omitted by OCR, and repaired `is` in the printed English quotation. The source visibly prints the middle fiscal year as `2000-2009`; this apparent source error is preserved and noted rather than silently changed to 2008-2009. Other repairs are confined to obvious OCR glyph and word-join damage. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Under Jayalalithaa’s rule, prices keep rising day after day like deadly poison. Conceited by the sweeping victory the people gave them in the parliamentary election, the AIADMK government refuses even to think about the suffering of the people. Its foremost concern is: How can we escape the Bengaluru disproportionate-assets case? Can someone at the Centre be won over? Will anyone come forward to help? For that purpose, anything may be done!

How many statements did they issue about prices when the DMK was in office? Today, one need hardly speak of the rise in the price of construction materials. From vegetables onward, whatever one takes up has become dearer. Does this government show concern and come forward to act? The answer is no.

On 14-10-2007, Jayalalithaa issued a statement comparing the prices of cement, sand, bricks, steel rods and blue metal under AIADMK rule with their higher prices under the DMK. She said the increase had weakened the entire construction industry and endangered the livelihood of lakhs of workers dependent upon it. The AIADMK even held a demonstration on 15-10-2007. Today, its government seems to think that the people have forgotten all that, or that no one will remind them.

In that statement, Jayalalithaa said cement cost Rs. 270 a bag. Do you know what steps the DMK government took to reduce it? We invited the owners of every cement manufacturing company for talks and asked them to lower the price. They agreed to reduce it by Rs. 20 a bag, and the news appeared immediately.

The government ordered the procurement and concessional sale of cement at Rs. 200 a bag. Through the Tamil Nadu Civil Supplies Corporation, 48,496 metric tonnes were sold up to 31-3-2008 in 2007-2008; 5,63,965 metric tonnes in the year printed in the source as 2000-2009; and 9,79,330 metric tonnes in 2009-2010. Because the government itself competed with private companies and sold at a reduced price, the open-market price also fell substantially.

Has the AIADMK government taken any such step during the past three years? Has it summoned even one cement producer for negotiations to reduce prices? It has time for covert bargaining to raise liquor prices, but why would it care about the price of cement?

Do you know how sharply cement prices rose in just fifteen days? According to a report in *The Hindu* on 19-6-2014, a bag that cost Rs. 285 on 31 May rose to Rs. 350 on 17-6-2014. And not only *The Hindu*. According to *Dinamalar* on 21-6-2014, a bag that cost Rs. 310 the previous week rose to Rs. 340 in the current week.

Did the AIADMK government not see these reports? When cement was sold at Rs. 200 a bag under DMK rule, today’s Chief Minister Jayalalithaa issued a statement calling for a protest. Now that a bag costs Rs. 340 or Rs. 350, what has she done to reduce the price and save the construction industry? Is cement the only commodity whose price has risen under this government?

Under the headline “Steel rod price rises by Rs. 3,300 a tonne,” *Dinamalar* reported on 21-6-2014: “As of last week, a tonne of steel rod sold for Rs. 48,600. It has now risen to Rs. 52,800. In an unprecedented development, the price of rods from major companies rose by Rs. 3,300 a tonne in a single week. Those in the industry fear that construction costs will rise steeply.” Did the AIADMK government not see this, or did it see and deliberately look away?

In a press release on these increases, the All India Builders’ Association said: “During the past ten days, the prices of all construction materials, including cement, blue metal and bricks, have risen by between 25 and 100 percent. Cement alone has risen by as much as Rs. 70 a bag. An ordinary home-builder now faces an increase of Rs. 300 to Rs. 400 per square foot. Government contracts, particularly those in municipal corporations and the Highways Department, were entered into before these increases and are now being implemented. Contractors must spend between 19 and 90 percent more to complete them. Consequently, most works have remained stalled and unable to proceed for the past ten days.”

According to *The Hindu* of 19-6-2014: “The price of cement has increased on an average by Rs. 60 per bag and that of steel has nearly doubled. ‘What is shocking is that the price of cement has increased dramatically in less than 10 days all over south India,’ said Suresh Krishna, Vice-President of Confederation of Real Estate Developers Association of India, Chennai Region.”

A newspaper report dated 18-6-2014, under the headline “Housing construction costs soar, material prices create difficulties,” quoted office-bearers of the Builders’ Association of India as saying that increases in cement, sand, blue metal and other construction materials had raised housing construction costs by up to Rs. 300 per square foot in a single week.

Cement had risen by as much as Rs. 70 a bag in one week, an unprecedented increase. Blue metal rose by 30 percent and sand by 50 percent. Housing construction costs consequently increased by Rs. 300 to Rs. 400 per square foot. Middle- and lower-income buyers would have to spend as much as Rs. 2 lakh more on a home. The rise in cement prices threatened to bring government and private construction projects across Tamil Nadu to a complete standstill. The Chief Minister needed to summon local producers and act to control prices. The organisations were planning hunger strikes and other protests in Chennai and district headquarters throughout Tamil Nadu to press these demands.

How many days have passed since that statement appeared? Has the government thought about it at all? Has it shown any concern? Under the DMK government, I summoned the owners of the cement companies to the Secretariat, spoke with them and acted to reduce prices. Why has no such action been taken now? Is there no will to spend time on these matters? Is there no way?

In a report published on 22-6-2014 under the headline “Cement and sand price rise stalls work, government intervention urged,” Venkatachalam, founding president of the Chennai Civil Engineers Association, said:

“The rise in the prices of cement, sand and blue metal threatens to halt every kind of construction work. Their prices in Tamil Nadu have risen to an unprecedented extent. Sand supplied at Rs. 300 a unit at a Public Works Department quarry is sold at the next stage for Rs. 2,800. It costs still more when it reaches the construction site. Even at the higher price, good-quality sand is not available on time. Sand brought from other districts is heavily adulterated by the time it reaches the market.

“Chennai and its surrounding areas require twenty thousand loads of blue metal a day. Quarrying has now been permitted at only four places. Added transport costs have therefore sharply increased the price. Only action by the Tamil Nadu government can resolve this problem. Otherwise, construction activity as a whole will come to a complete standstill.”
They have said that these problems can be solved only if the Tamil Nadu government acts. Has it acted? If not, why has it failed to do so?
Is the government’s answer: “We won thirty-seven seats in the parliamentary election. Why should we now worry about rising prices?”
Does it imagine that every problem will be solved merely by opening “Amma” canteens and pharmacies and entering the salt trade?
With affection,
M.K.
